source: ExchangeRate-API (open.er-api.com) mid-market rates 16:30 UTC, cross-referenced with Wise and Trading Economics
baseNote: Rates show ZMW per 1 unit of foreign currency. ↑ ZMW = Kwacha weakening (depreciating).
mpRateNote: Monetary Policy Rate held at 13.50% — reduced 75bps from 14.25% on Feb 11, 2026. BOZ held rate at May 2026 review. Next review: August 2026.

USD.note: OpenExchangeRates Jun 19: 1 USD = 17.824 ZMW. Wise shows 1 ZMW = 0.05555 USD (1 USD = 18.00). Kwacha slightly softer from 17.784 (Jun 18). Week high 17.46, low 17.82.

GBP.note: 1 GBP = 23.569 ZMW (OpenExRates Jun 19). Sterling weaker against kwacha from 23.894 (Jun 18).

EUR.note: 1 EUR = 20.446 ZMW (OpenExRates Jun 19). Euro weaker vs kwacha from 20.663 (Jun 18).

ZAR.note: 1 ZAR = 1.085 ZMW (OpenExRates Jun 19). Rand weaker vs kwacha from 1.101 (Jun 18). 1 ZMW = 0.922 ZAR.

BWP.note: Botswana pula. OpenExRates cross: 1 BWP = 1.293 ZMW (17.824 / 13.785).

trendAnalysis.direction: Kwacha mixed — softer vs USD (17.824 from 17.784) but firmer vs GBP, EUR, ZAR. USD strength driving cross-rates.
drivers: Record copper output 890K tonnes (2025) — strong export earnings
Record maize harvest >4M tonnes — reduced food import needs
Debt restructuring 94% complete — sovereign risk premium collapsing
IMF successor programme talks scheduled July 2026 — staff visit confirmed
MPR at 13.5% — high real yields attracting carry trade inflows
Yuan mining tax corridor reducing USD demand
Gross international reserves at historic high of US$6.5 billion
trendAnalysis.outlook: Kwacha showing mixed signals — weakening against broadly strong USD but gaining on crosses. Profit-taking from Jun 11 peak (17.46) continues. Copper prices and IMF programme progress remain key catalysts. BOZ leaning against excessive strength to protect export competitiveness.
trendAnalysis.projection3m: USD/ZMW 16.50–18.00 range. Expect continued appreciation bias but with increased volatility ahead of August 2026 elections.
trendAnalysis.projection6m: USD/ZMW 16.00–19.00 range. Post-election clarity determines direction — policy continuity supports kwacha, fiscal slippage reverses gains.
